text.title: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the prompt-engineering-fundamentals methodology. Sources: Anthropic prompt-engineering documentation; OpenAI prompt-engineering guide; Google Gemini prompt design strategies.

rule.statement: Every prompt MUST include at minimum: a task description (what to do) and an output format specification (what structure is required). Context, system role, examples, and constraints are added only when they measurably improve reliability.
rule.rationale: Minimal prompts are easier to debug. Adding components one at a time lets you isolate which change caused a quality improvement or regression.

rule.statement: Start with zero-shot. Add few-shot examples only when zero-shot produces wrong patterns or formatting. Add chain-of-thought only when reasoning is demonstrably failing. Escalate complexity only after the simpler approach fails.
rule.rationale: Each technique adds tokens and latency. The cheapest technique that achieves the target quality is the right one for production.

rule.statement: Few-shot examples MUST: cover representative diversity, include at least one edge case, follow a consistent format, and number no more than 2–5. Conflicting examples MUST NOT appear in the same prompt.
rule.rationale: Too many examples waste tokens and can confuse the model by emphasizing the pattern over the instruction. Consistent format prevents the model from averaging conflicting signals.

rule.statement: For Claude, MUST use XML tags to delimit sections (context, task, output) and user input. For GPT-4, MUST use markdown headers and numbered lists. NEVER use Claude-style XML tags in a GPT-4 prompt without testing — the model may treat them as literal text.

rationale.source: Brown et al. — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (arXiv:2005.14165)

rule.rationale: Instruction-following behavior is trained differently per model family. Structural conventions that boost Claude reliability may be neutral or negative for GPT-4.

rule.statement: Validate that the produced `playbook-step` matches the schema in `content/02-output-contract.xml` before handing off (rule r5).
rule.rationale: Mechanical validation prevents downstream consumers from receiving malformed artefacts; the validator script enforces this at PR time.
